question_context: Testing whether a coin is loaded (75% heads vs 50% fair coin). What sample size is needed for 80% power at alpha=0.05 one-sided?
r_code: pwr.p.test(h = ES.h(p1 = 0.75, p2 = 0.50), sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = "greater")
source_description: pwr vignette: Getting started with the pwr package - one-sample proportion test

question_context: Comparing alcohol consumption rates between male and female undergraduates (55% vs 50%). What sample size per group is needed for 80% power?
r_code: pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(p1 = 0.55, p2 = 0.50), sig.level = 0.05, power = .80)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample proportion test with small effect

question_context: Comparing alcohol consumption rates between male and female undergraduates (10% vs 5%). What sample size per group is needed for 80% power?
r_code: pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(p1 = 0.10, p2 = 0.05), sig.level = 0.05, power = .80)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample proportion test with larger effect

question_context: Survey with 543 male and 675 female participants. What is the power to detect a small effect (h=0.2) in proportions?
r_code: pwr.2p2n.test(h = 0.2, n1 = 543, n2 = 675, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample proportion test with unequal sample sizes

question_context: Comparing mean coffee shop purchase prices by gender with n=30 per group. What is the power to detect a medium effect (d=0.5)?
r_code: pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample t-test power calculation

question_context: What sample size per group is needed for 80% power to detect a medium effect (d=0.5) in a two-sample t-test?
r_code: pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, power = 0.80, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample t-test sample size

question_context: Detecting a 75-cent difference in coffee purchases (SD=$2.25). What sample size is needed per group for 80% power?
r_code:
d <- 0.75/2.25
pwr.t.test(d = d, power = 0.80, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Two-sample t-test with specific effect size

question_context: Testing if mean coffee purchase exceeds $3 (one-sided). How many transactions needed for 90% power with d=0.222?
r_code:
d <- 0.50/2.25
pwr.t.test(d = d, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.90, alternative = "greater", type = "one.sample")
source_description: pwr vignette: One-sample t-test

question_context: 24 boys tested for 40-yard dash improvement after rope-jumping program (SD=0.25s, expected improvement=0.08s). What is the power of a paired t-test?
r_code: pwr.t.test(n = 24, d = 0.08 / 0.25, type = "paired", alternative = "greater")
source_description: pwr vignette: Paired t-test power

question_context: How many pairs needed for 80% power in the rope-jumping study (paired t-test, d=0.32, one-sided)?
r_code: pwr.t.test(d = 0.08 / 0.25, power = 0.8, type = "paired", alternative = "greater")
source_description: pwr vignette: Paired t-test sample size

question_context: Package design preference study: 4 designs tested on 100 consumers, expecting 3/8 preference for one design. What power does a chi-square GOF test have?
r_code:
null <- rep(0.25, 4)
alt <- c(3/8, rep((5/8)/3, 3))
ES.w1(null, alt)  # w = 0.2887
pwr.chisq.test(w = 0.2887, N = 100, df = 3, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Chi-square goodness of fit

question_context: How many consumers needed for 80% power in package design preference study (chi-square GOF, w=0.2887, df=3)?
r_code: pwr.chisq.test(w = 0.2887, df = 3, power = 0.8, sig.level = 0.05)
source_description: pwr vignette: Chi-square GOF sample size

question_context: Gender vs daily flossing behavior (2x2 contingency table, w=0.2182). How many students for 90% power at alpha=0.01?
r_code: pwr.chisq.test(w = 0.2182, power = 0.9, df = 1, sig.level = 0.01)
source_description: pwr vignette: Chi-square test of association

question_context: Detecting a small positive correlation (r=0.1) between weight change and initial weight. Sample size for 80% power at alpha=0.01 one-sided?
r_code: pwr.r.test(r = 0.1, sig.level = 0.01, power = 0.8, alternative = "greater")
source_description: pwr vignette: Correlation test sample size

question_context: Three fuel types tested for MPG differences using one-way ANOVA. How many trials per fuel type for 90% power at alpha=0.01 with medium effect (f=0.25)?
r_code: pwr.anova.test(k = 3, f = 0.25, sig.level = 0.01, power = 0.9)
source_description: pwr vignette: Balanced one-way ANOVA

question_context: Predicting first-year GPA from SAT scores and class rank (R-squared=0.30, 2 predictors). Sample size for 80% power at alpha=0.001?
r_code: pwr.f2.test(u = 2, f2 = 0.3/(1 - 0.3), sig.level = 0.001, power = 0.8)
source_description: pwr vignette: Multiple regression (general linear model)

question_context: Major depression treatment trial (Binneman et al. 2008): HAM-D score change, delta=3, SD=7.5, alpha=0.10 one-sided, power=83%. Design a 2-analysis group sequential study with O'Brien-Fleming spending.
r_code:
n <- nNormal(delta1 = 3, delta0 = 0, ratio = 1, sd = 7.5, alpha = 0.1, beta = 0.17)
x <- gsDesign(k = 2, test.type = 4, alpha = 0.1, beta = 0.17, timing = 0.5, sfu = sfLDOF, sfl = sfLDOF, delta1 = 3, delta0 = 0, endpoint = "normal", n.fix = n)
x <- toInteger(x)
result: Interim at N=59: efficacy Z=2.2209 (p=0.0132), futility Z=-0.2304 (p=0.5911). Final at N=134: Z=1.3047. Power if delta=3: 83.5%
source_description: gsDesign vignette: A gentle introduction to group sequential design - Depression trial

question_context: COVID-19 vaccine efficacy trial: VE1=70%, VE0=30%, 3:1 randomization, alpha=0.025 one-sided, 90% power, 3 analyses with HSD spending. Design group sequential trial with exact binomial bounds.
r_code:
x <- gsSurv(k = 3, test.type = 4, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.1, timing = c(.45, .7), sfu = sfHSD, sfupar = -3, sfl = sfHSD, sflpar = -3, lambdaC = .002, eta = .0001, hr = 1 - 0.7, hr0 = 1 - 0.3, R = 8, T = 24, minfup = 16, ratio = 3)
xx <- toInteger(x)
xb <- toBinomialExact(x)
result: Total N=3606, 68 events required. Analysis 1 (30 events): efficacy bound a=12, futility b=21. Analysis 2 (47 events): a=23, b=30. Final (68 events): a=37, b=38.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Vaccine efficacy trial design with exact binomial bounds

question_context: SPUTNIK COVID-19 vaccine trial: updating binomial bounds based on actual interim data (20 events at first database lock, 78 events at second with 16 in experimental group).
r_code: ebUpdate <- toBinomialExact(xx, observedEvents = c(20, 78))
result: Analysis 1 (20 cases): efficacy bound=6 (VE>=0.86), futility=16 (VE<=-0.33). Analysis 2 (78 cases): efficacy=44 (VE>=0.57), futility=45. With 16 experimental events, efficacy crossed (16 << 44).
source_description: gsDesign vignette: SPUTNIK vaccine trial real-data update

question_context: How many events are needed for 90% power with HR=0.7 at alpha=0.025 one-sided?
r_code: nEvents(hr = 0.7, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.1, r = 1, tbl = TRUE)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Survival endpoint design - Schoenfeld formula

question_context: Lachin-Foulkes fixed design: control median=8 months, HR=0.7, 12-month enrollment, 16-month minimum follow-up, dropout rate 0.001/month. What total sample size and events are needed for 90% power?
r_code: Nlf <- nSurv(lambdaC = log(2) / 8, hr = 0.7, eta = 0.001, T = 28, minfup = 16, ratio = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.1)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Lachin-Foulkes method for survival endpoint

question_context: Group sequential survival design: 2 analyses, control median=8 months, HR=0.7, enrollment 12 months, minimum follow-up 16 months.
r_code: lfgs <- gsSurv(k = 2, lambdaC = log(2) / 8, hr = 0.7, eta = 0.001, T = 28, minfup = 16, ratio = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.1) %>% toInteger()
result: Interim (172 events, month 13): efficacy HR ~0.658. Final (344 events, month 28): efficacy HR ~0.808. Power if HR=0.7: 90.06%
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Group sequential survival design with Lachin-Foulkes

question_context: Sample size for 2:1 randomization with HR=0.8 based on Z-value approach. How many events are needed?
r_code: hrz2n(hr = 0.8, z = qnorm(0.025), ratio = 2)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Survival events from HR and Z-value

question_context: Superiority trial comparing success rates 0.2 vs 0.1 with 2:1 randomization, alpha=0.025, 85% power. What total sample size is needed?
r_code: nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, ratio = 2, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15) %>% ceiling()
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Binomial two-arm superiority trial

question_context: Compare sample size requirements using risk-difference, risk-ratio, and odds-ratio scales for p1=0.2 vs p2=0.1.
r_code:
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, ratio = 0.5, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, scale = "Difference")
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, ratio = 0.5, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, scale = "RR")
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, ratio = 0.5, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, scale = "OR")
result: Risk Difference: N=525, Risk-Ratio: N=525, Odds-Ratio: N=489
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Binomial sample size across effect scales

question_context: Non-inferiority vs superiority vs super-superiority sample sizes: p1=0.2, p2=0.1, with different delta0 margins.
r_code:
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, ratio = 0.5, delta0 = 0)
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, ratio = 0.5, delta0 = -0.02)
nBinomial(p1 = 0.2, p2 = 0.1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.15, ratio = 0.5, delta0 = 0.02)
result: Superiority (delta0=0): N=525. Non-inferiority (delta0=-0.02): N=375. Super-superiority (delta0=0.02): N=796.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Binomial design comparison across margins

question_context: Two-sample normal endpoint with unequal SDs (sigma1=1.6, sigma2=1.25), delta=0.8, 2:1 randomization, alpha=0.025 one-sided, 90% power. What sample size is needed?
r_code: nNormal(delta1 = 0.8, sd = 1.6, sd2 = 1.25, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.1, ratio = 2)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Two-sample normal sample size with unequal variances

question_context: Power at N=200 for two-sample normal test with unequal SDs (1.6, 1.25), delta=0.8, 2:1 randomization?
r_code: nNormal(delta1 = 0.8, sd = 1.6, sd2 = 1.25, alpha = 0.025, n = 200, ratio = 2)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Normal endpoint power at fixed N

question_context: Group sequential design with 2 analyses for normal endpoint (unequal SDs, delta=0.8, 2:1 randomization, 90% power).
r_code: d <- gsDesign(k = 2, n.fix = nNormal(delta1 = 0.8, sd = 1.6, sd2 = 1.25, alpha = 0.025, beta = .1, ratio = 2), delta1 = 0.8)
result: Total N=172. Interim (N=86): efficacy Z=2.7500. Final (N=172): efficacy Z=1.9811. Power=90%.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Group sequential design for normal endpoint

question_context: Superiority survival trial: control median=12 months, HR=0.75, 36-month study, 12-month minimum follow-up, varied enrollment rates. Fixed design (no interims).
result: Expected total events: 507.15. Expected sample size: 775.03. Power: 90%.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Fixed survival design with stepped enrollment

question_context: 3-analysis group sequential survival trial: HR=0.75, interims at 25% and 75% of events, O'Brien-Fleming efficacy, HSD(-7) futility, 85% power.
r_code: x <- gsSurv(k = 3, timing = c(0.25, 0.75), R = c(1, 2, 3, 4), gamma = c(1, 1.5, 2.5, 4), eta = 0.001, minfup = 12, T = 36, lambdaC = log(2) / 12, hr = 0.75, hr0 = 1, beta = 0.15, alpha = 0.025, sfu = sfLDOF, sfl = sfHSD, sflpar = -7)
result: N=676, 443 events. IA1 (111 events): Z=4.33 efficacy, Z=-1.70 futility. IA2 (332 events): Z=2.34. Final (443 events): Z=2.01. Power=85%.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Group sequential survival trial with futility monitoring

question_context: Conditional and predictive power at interim 2 with observed Z=2.0 in group sequential survival trial.
r_code:
Z <- c(0.25, 2)
gsCP(x = xu, i = 2, zi = Z[2])$upper$prob
prior <- normalGrid(mu = log(0.75) / 2, sigma = sqrt(20 / 443))
gsPP(x = xu, i = 2, zi = Z[2], theta = prior$z, wgts = prior$wgts)
result: Conditional power: 0.66 under observed trend, 0.30 under null, 0.78 under alternative. Predictive power (Bayesian) = 0.641.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Conditional and predictive power at interim

question_context: Time-to-event trial (HR=0.7, median control=12mo, 24mo follow-up): design 3-analysis group sequential study and compute conditional power when interim p=0.04.
r_code:
design <- gsSurv(hr = 0.7, lambdaC = log(2) / 12, minfup = 24, T = 36) %>% toInteger()
cp <- gsCP(x = update, i = 1, zi = -qnorm(0.04))
result: Design: 464 participants, 353 events, 90% power. At interim with p=0.04: observed HR=0.7235. Conditional power=0.899 under observed, 0.937 under alternative HR.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Conditional power and conditional error

question_context: Cure model calendar-based design for time-to-event with cure rate: cure_rate=0.50, 24-month survival=0.65, HR=0.70, 48-month study, 12-month enrollment.
result: Scenario 1: IA1 (month 14, 123 events): Z=2.91 efficacy, Z=-1.40 futility, HR at bound=0.591. IA2 (month 24, 228 events): Z=2.72, HR=0.697. Final (month 48, 337 events): Z=2.00, HR=0.804. Power=90%.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Cure model calendar-based trial design

question_context: Single-arm response rate monitoring: historical baseline 10%, target 35% response, alpha=0.08, beta=0.20, sample 10-25 patients. Design SPRT boundaries.
r_code: b <- binomialSPRT(p0 = .1, p1 = .35, alpha = .08, beta = .2, minn = 10, maxn = 25)
result: At n=10: reject null if >=4 responses, accept if <=1. At n=25: reject if >=7, accept if <=4. At true rate 10%: futility prob=0.94, efficacy=0.04, avg N=12.1. At true rate 35%: futility=0.12, efficacy=0.84, avg N=12.5.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Binomial SPRT for response rate monitoring

question_context: Safety adverse event monitoring: baseline AE rate 4%, unacceptable rate 10%, alpha=0.04, beta=0.20, monitoring 4-75 patients.
r_code: safety_design <- binomialSPRT(p0 = .04, p1 = .1, alpha = .04, beta = .2, minn = 4, maxn = 75)
result: At 4% AE rate: low-rate prob=0.769, high-rate prob=0.019, avg N=46.4. At 10%: low=0.155, high=0.516, avg N=52.8. At 16%: low=0.020, high=0.930, avg N=34.2.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Binomial SPRT for safety monitoring

question_context: Basic group sequential design: fixed design requires N=200 for 90% power at alpha=0.025 one-sided. Design a 3-analysis GSD with default spending functions.
r_code:
x <- gsDesign(n.fix = 200)
gsBoundSummary(x)
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Package overview - basic GSD

question_context: Converting continuous event counts to integers in a time-to-event GSD: HR=0.74, 1:1 randomization.
r_code:
x <- gsSurv(ratio = 1, hr = .74)
y <- x |> toInteger()
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Integer sample size rounding for time-to-event

question_context: Binary endpoint GSD with 2:1 randomization: p1=0.2, p2=0.1, alpha=0.025, 80% power, O'Brien-Fleming spending, interims at 50% and 75%.
r_code:
n.fix <- nBinomial(p1 = .2, p2 = .1, alpha = .025, beta = .2, ratio = 2)
xb <- gsDesign(alpha = .025, beta = .2, n.fix = n.fix, test.type = 1, sfu = sfLDOF, timing = c(.5, .75))
x_integer <- toInteger(xb, ratio = 2)
result: Fixed N=429.88. GSD integer N: 219, 329, 441. Information fractions: 0.497, 0.746, 1.00. Cumulative power: 0.165, 0.537, 0.803.
source_description: gsDesign vignette: Integer rounding for binary endpoint GSD

question_context: Standard bioequivalence study: 2x2 crossover, intra-subject CV=0.30, true ratio=0.95, alpha=0.05, target power=0.80. What sample size is needed?
r_code: sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.30)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Average Bioequivalence - basic sample size

question_context: Compare sample sizes for 2x2x2, 2x2x3, 2x3x3, and 2x2x4 replicate crossover designs at CV=0.30.
r_code:
sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.30, design = "2x2x2")
sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.30, design = "2x2x3")
sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.30, design = "2x3x3")
sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.30, design = "2x2x4")
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE replicate design comparison

question_context: Pilot study with 16 subjects showed CV=0.20 and theta0=0.92. Accounting for uncertainty using upper 80% CL for CV, what sample size is needed?
r_code:
CL.upper <- CVCL(CV = 0.20, df = 14, side = "upper", alpha = 0.20)[["upper CL"]]  # 0.244
sampleN.TOST(CV = CL.upper, theta0 = 0.92)
result: Conservative approach: n=40 (power=0.817). Point estimate approach: n=28 (power=0.823).
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE with pilot study uncertainty

question_context: Expected power (statistical assurance) accounting for uncertainty in both CV and theta0 from a 16-subject pilot (CV=0.20, theta0=0.92).
r_code: expsampleN.TOST(CV = 0.20, theta0 = 0.92, prior.type = "both", prior.parm = list(m = 16, design = "2x2x2"), details = FALSE)
result: Sample size: 54 subjects (expected power = 0.802). Compare: CV uncertainty only: n=30 (0.806), theta0 uncertainty only: n=46 (0.805).
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE statistical assurance approach

question_context: Blood pressure comparison of two antihypertensives: 2x2x2 crossover, additive model (untransformed), residual SD=20 mmHg, expected difference=-5, equivalence margins +/-15 mmHg.
r_code: sampleN.TOST(CV = 20, theta0 = -5, theta1 = -15, theta2 = +15, logscale = FALSE, design = "2x2x2")
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE additive model for blood pressure

question_context: Extract CV from published confidence interval (2x2x4 design, n=26, CI 0.8323-1.0392).
r_code: CVfromCI(lower = 0.8323, upper = 1.0392, design = "2x2x4", n = 26)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE - extracting CV from literature CI

question_context: Post-hoc power analysis of failed BE study: theta0=0.90, CV=0.25, imbalanced groups n=(13,12).
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE post-hoc power of failed study

question_context: Pool CV estimates from pilot (n=16, CV=0.20) and pivotal (n=25, CV=0.25) bioequivalence studies.
r_code:
data <- data.frame(CV = c(0.20, 0.25), n = c(16, 25), design = c("2x2x2", "2x2x2"))
CVpooled(data, alpha = 0.20)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: ABE pooling CVs from multiple studies

question_context: EMA reference-scaled ABE (ABEL) for highly variable drug: CV=0.55, 2x3x3 partial replicate design, theta0=0.90, alpha=0.05, power=0.80.
r_code: sampleN.scABEL(CV = 0.55)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE - EMA ABEL for highly variable drugs

question_context: Health Canada ABEL: CV=0.55 (higher CVcap at 0.57382 vs EMA 0.50).
r_code: sampleN.scABEL(CV = 0.55, regulator = "HC")
result: Sample size: 39 subjects (lower than EMA's 42 due to higher cap)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE - Health Canada regulatory framework

question_context: GCC reference-scaled ABE with fixed widened limits for CV=0.55.
r_code: sampleN.scABEL(CV = 0.55, regulator = "GCC")
result: Sample size: 75 subjects (much larger due to fixed limits 0.75-1.333 for CV>0.30)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE - Gulf Cooperation Council

question_context: FDA reference-scaled ABE: CV=0.55 with unlimited scaling.
r_code: sampleN.RSABE(CV = 0.55)
result: Sample size: 30 subjects (substantially lower than EMA/HC/GCC due to unlimited scaling)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE - FDA unlimited scaling

question_context: Narrow Therapeutic Index Drug (NTID): CV_w=0.125, heteroscedastic (variance ratio=2.5), 2x2x4 design.
r_code:
CV <- signif(CVp2CV(CV = 0.125, ratio = 2.5), 4)
n <- sampleN.NTID(CV = CV, details = FALSE)[["Sample size"]]
suppressMessages(power.NTID(CV = CV, n = n, details = TRUE))
result: Sample size: 38 subjects. Power components: BE=0.816, scaled=0.938, ABE=1.000, ratio=0.858.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE for narrow therapeutic index drugs

question_context: Type I error inflation detection in ABEL framework: CV=0.35, 2x2x4 design.
r_code:
CV <- 0.35
res <- data.frame(n = NA, CV = CV, TIE = NA)
res$n <- sampleN.scABEL(CV = CV, design = "2x2x4", print = FALSE, details = FALSE)[["Sample size"]]
U <- scABEL(CV = CV)[["upper"]]
res$TIE <- power.scABEL(CV = CV, n = res$n, theta0 = U, design = "2x2x4")
result: n=34. TIE = 0.0656 (inflated from nominal 0.05). Adjusted alpha = 0.0363 controls TIE.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE Type I error inflation detection

question_context: Adjusted alpha sample size for ABEL with maintained power: CV=0.35, 2x2x4 design.
r_code: sampleN.scABEL.ad(CV = 0.35, design = "2x2x4")
result: n=38 with adjusted alpha=0.0361 achieves target 0.81 power while controlling TIE at 0.05.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: RSABE adjusted alpha sample size

question_context: Power analysis sensitivity for ABE study: CV=0.20, theta0=0.92. What are critical parameter values?
r_code: pa.ABE(CV = 0.20, theta0 = 0.92)
result: Planned: n=28, power=0.8227. Critical values where power drops to ~0.70: CV=0.2377, theta0=0.9001, n=21.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Power analysis sensitivity for ABE

question_context: Power analysis sensitivity for EMA ABEL (highly variable drug): CV=0.55, theta0=0.90, 2x3x3 design.
r_code: pa.scABE(CV = 0.55)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Power analysis sensitivity for RSABE

question_context: Power analysis for ABEL with 2x2x4 design: CV=0.40.
r_code: pa.scABE(CV = 0.40, design = "2x2x4")
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Power analysis for 4-period ABEL design

question_context: Non-inferiority study: 2x2 crossover, CV=0.25, margin=0.80, true ratio=0.95, alpha=0.025, 80% power.
r_code: sampleN.noninf(CV = 0.25)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Non-inferiority basic sample size

question_context: Bracketing approach for modified release formulation: Compare Cmin (efficacy, margin=0.80, CV=0.35) and Cmax (safety, margin=1.25, CV=0.20) in 2x2x4 design.
r_code:
sampleN.noninf(design = "2x2x4", margin = 0.80, theta0 = 0.95, CV = 0.35)  # Cmin
sampleN.noninf(design = "2x2x4", margin = 1.25, theta0 = 1.05, CV = 0.20)  # Cmax
result: Cmin drives sample size: n=32 (power=0.808). Cmax: n=12 (power=0.841). Cmax overpowered at n=32 (power=0.998).
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Non-inferiority bracketing approach for modified release

question_context: Dose-proportionality study with modified Fibonacci escalation: 3 dose levels (10, 20, 33.3), CV=0.20, beta0=1.02.
r_code: sampleN.dp(CV = 0.20, doses = c(10, 20, 33.3), beta0 = 1.02)
result: Sample size: 15 subjects, power = 0.808 in 3x3 Latin square crossover.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Dose-proportionality - 3 dose levels

question_context: Dose-proportionality with 4 dose levels (10, 20, 33.3, 50): CV=0.20, beta0=1.02.
r_code:
doses <- c(10, 20, 33.3, 50)
sampleN.dp(CV = 0.20, doses = doses, beta0 = 1.02)
result: Sample size: 16 subjects, power = 0.867. Acceptance range: 0.8614-1.1386.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Dose-proportionality - 4 dose levels

question_context: Dose-proportionality with incomplete block design: 5 doses, 3 periods per subject, CV=0.20.
r_code:
doses <- c(10, 20, 33.3, 50, 66.7)
dm <- crossdes::balmin.RMD(5, 10, 3)
sampleN.dp(CV = 0.20, doses = doses, beta0 = 1.02, design = "IBD", dm = dm)
result: Sample size: 30 subjects, power = 0.899 in IBD (5x10x3).
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Dose-proportionality - incomplete block design

question_context: Wide dose range (powers of 2: 1,4,16,64,256), CV=0.30. Sample size for dose-proportionality crossover?
r_code:
doses <- 2^(seq(0, 8, 2))
sampleN.dp(CV = 0.30, doses = doses, beta0 = 1.02, design = "crossover")
result: Sample size: 70 subjects, power = 0.810 in 5x5 Latin square. Very tight acceptance range: 0.960-1.040.
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Dose-proportionality - wide dose range

question_context: Dose-proportionality with liberal equivalence margins (theta1=0.75) for wide dose range exploratory setting.
r_code:
doses <- 2^(seq(0, 8, 2))
sampleN.dp(CV = 0.30, doses = doses, beta0 = 1.02, design = "crossover", theta1 = 0.75)
source_description: PowerTOST vignette: Dose-proportionality - liberal margins

question_context: Pet ownership and life satisfaction: 2 between-subject groups (control=24, pet=26.2), SD=6.4, n=100 per group. Simulation-based power.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "2b", n = 100, mu = c(24, 26.2), sd = 6.4, label_list = list("condition" = c("control", "pet")))
power_result <- ANOVA_power(design_result, nsims = 1000, seed = 1234)
result: Power = 68.8% (95% CI: 62.8%-74.2%). Need n=180 per group for ~90% power.
source_description: Superpower vignette: Introduction - two-group between-subjects

question_context: Voice assistant study: 2b*2w design (human/robot voice x cheerful/sad), n=40, means=[1.03,1.41,0.98,1.01], SD=1.03, r=0.8.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "2b*2w", n = 40, mu = c(1.03, 1.41, 0.98, 1.01), sd = 1.03, r = 0.8, label_list = list("voice" = c("human", "robot"), "emotion" = c("cheerful", "sad")))
ANOVA_power(design_result, alpha = 0.05, nsims = 1000, seed = 1234)
result: Voice main effect: 18.8% power. Emotion main effect: 79.6% power. Interaction: 68.0% power.
source_description: Superpower vignette: Introduction - mixed 2x2 factorial design

question_context: Exact power calculation for pet ownership study with n=180 per group.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "2b", n = 180, mu = c(24, 26.2), sd = 6.4, label_list = list("condition" = c("control", "pet")))
ANOVA_exact(design_result, verbose = FALSE)$main_results$power
source_description: Superpower vignette: Exact power calculation

question_context: One-way ANCOVA: 3 groups (means 400,450,500), SD=100, 3 covariates explaining 25% variance. Sample size for 80% power using approximate method.
r_code: power_oneway_ancova(mu = c(400,450,500), n_cov = 3, sd = 100, r2 = .25, alpha_level = .05, beta_level = .2, round_up = TRUE, type = "approx")
source_description: Superpower vignette: One-way ANCOVA power (Cohen approximate)

question_context: One-way ANCOVA with exact method (Shieh): same scenario as above.
r_code: power_oneway_ancova(mu = c(400,450,500), n_cov = 3, sd = 100, r2 = .25, alpha_level = .05, beta_level = .2, round_up = TRUE, type = "exact")
source_description: Superpower vignette: One-way ANCOVA power (Shieh exact)

question_context: Factorial 2b*3b ANCOVA: 6 groups (means 400,450,500,400,500,600), 3 covariates, r2=0.25. Sample size for 80% power for each effect.
r_code: ANCOVA_analytic(design = "2b*3b", mu = c(400, 450, 500, 400, 500, 600), n_cov = 3, sd = 100, r2 = .25, alpha_level = .05, beta_level = .2, round_up = TRUE)
result: Factor A: N=102, power=81.03%. Factor B: N=30, power=86.11%. A*B interaction: N=180, power=80.05%.
source_description: Superpower vignette: Factorial ANCOVA analytic power

question_context: ANCOVA contrast: two-group comparison (means 0,1), 1 covariate (r2=0.20), n=15 per group.
r_code: ANCOVA_contrast(cmat = c(-1,1), n = 15, mu = c(0,1), sd = 1, r2 = .2, n_cov = 1)
source_description: Superpower vignette: ANCOVA contrast power

question_context: Three between-subject conditions (control=24, cat=26.2, dog=26.6), SD=6.4, n=50 per group. Power for one-way ANOVA and pairwise comparisons.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "3b", n = 50, mu = c(24, 26.2, 26.6), sd = 6.4, label_list = list("condition" = c("control", "cat", "dog")))
ANOVA_exact2(design_result, emm = TRUE)
result: ANOVA power ~47.5%. Pairwise: control-cat 39.8%, control-dog 52.1%, cat-dog 6.1%.
source_description: Superpower vignette: Three-group between-subjects ANOVA

question_context: Independent samples t-test, d=0.5, n=64 per group. Find the optimal alpha that minimizes total error rate.
r_code: res <- optimal_alpha(power_function = "power.t.test(delta = .5, sd = 1, n=64, sig.level = x, type='two.sample', alternative='two.sided')$power")
result: Optimal alpha = 0.0997 (vs conventional 0.05). Corresponding beta = 0.1217. Combined error = 11.07% (vs 12.5% conventional).
source_description: Superpower vignette: Optimal alpha for independent t-test

question_context: T-test with Type I errors 4x more costly: d=0.5, n=100 per group. Find weighted optimal alpha.
r_code: res3 <- optimal_alpha(power_function = "power.t.test(delta = .5, sd = 1, n=100, sig.level = x, type='two.sample', alternative='two.sided')$power", error = "minimal", costT1T2 = 4)
result: Optimal alpha = 0.0192 (much stricter). Beta = 0.1212. Weighted error = 3.96%.
source_description: Superpower vignette: Optimal alpha with asymmetric error costs

question_context: Comprehensive ANOVA compromise: 2b*2w design for voice assistant study. Find optimal alpha for each effect.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "2b*2w", n = 40, mu = c(1.03, 1.41, 0.98, 1.01), sd = 1.03, r = 0.8)
comp_res = ANOVA_compromise(design_result, emm = TRUE)
result: Voice (f=0.116): optimal alpha=0.279, beta=0.507. Emotion (f=0.317): alpha=0.104, beta=0.125. Interaction (f=0.271): alpha=0.142, beta=0.183.
source_description: Superpower vignette: ANOVA compromise power analysis

question_context: 2w*3w repeated measures design: cheerful/sad x human/robot/cartoon voice, n=40, means=[0.3,0,0.5,0.3,0,0], SD=2, r=0.8. Power for pairwise contrasts via emmeans.
r_code:
design_result <- ANOVA_design(design = "2w*3w", n = 40, mu = c(0.3, 0, 0.5, 0.3, 0, 0), sd = 2, r = 0.8)
exact_result <- ANOVA_exact(design_result, alpha_level = 0.05, verbose = FALSE, emm = TRUE, contrast_type = "pairwise")
result: Pairwise power ranged from 16.4% to 68.4%. Largest effect (cheerful/cartoon vs sad/cartoon): 68.37%.
source_description: Superpower vignette: emmeans pairwise comparisons power

question_context: Custom linear contrast: robot voice vs averaged sad human conditions in 2w*3w design.
r_code:
custom_contrast <- contrast(exact_result$emmeans$emmeans, list(robot_vs_sad_human = c(0, 0, 0, 1, -0.5, -0.5)))
emmeans_power(custom_contrast)
source_description: Superpower vignette: Custom contrast power via emmeans

question_context: Equivalence testing: are voice condition differences within +/-0.3 margin?
r_code: emmeans_power(pairs(simple_condition_effects, side = "equivalence", delta = 0.3)[2])
result: Power for equivalence test = 31.0% (human voice comparison)
source_description: Superpower vignette: Equivalence testing power via emmeans

question_context: Mobile colposcope trial: estimating sensitivity=75%, prevalence=15%, N=250. What precision (CI width) is achieved?
r_code: prec_sens(sens = .75, prev = .15, ntot = 250, method = "wilson")
source_description: presize vignette: Sensitivity precision with fixed N

question_context: How many total participants for +/-5% CI width for sensitivity estimate (sens=0.75, prev=0.15)?
r_code: prec_sens(sens = .75, prev = .15, conf.width = .1, method = "wilson")
result: Required total sample: 1,904 participants. Positive cases needed: 286. 95% CI: 69.7%-79.7%.
source_description: presize vignette: Sample size for target sensitivity precision

question_context: Specificity precision: spec=0.75, prevalence=0.15, N=250 using exact method.
r_code: prec_spec(spec = .75, prev = .15, ntot = 250, method = "exact")
source_description: presize vignette: Specificity precision with fixed N

question_context: Precision of a mean estimate: mean=60, SD=10, N=40.
r_code: prec_mean(60, sd = 10, n = 40)
source_description: presize vignette: Mean precision calculation

question_context: How many subjects needed for CI width of 5 around a mean (mean=60, SD=10)?
r_code: prec_mean(60, sd = 10, conf.width = 5)
source_description: presize vignette: Sample size for target mean precision

question_context: Sensitivity precision across a range of sensitivity values (0.50 to 0.95) with N=250 and prevalence=0.15.
r_code: prec_sens(sens = seq(.5, .95, .05), prev = .15, ntot = 250, method = "wilson")
result: At 50% sensitivity: CI width=0.303. At 75%: 0.266. At 95%: 0.156. Higher sensitivity gives narrower CI.
source_description: presize vignette: Multi-scenario sensitivity analysis

question_context: Reproducing Diggle, Liang, Zeger (1994) Table: longitudinal linear model power with exchangeable correlation, 3 visits at t=(0,2,5), d=0.5, one-sided alpha=0.05, 80% power.
r_code:
n = 3; t = c(0,2,5)
rho = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8)
sigma2 = c(100, 200, 300)
tab.diggle = outer(rho, sigma2, Vectorize(function(rho, sigma2){
  ceiling(diggle.linear.power(d=0.5, t=t, sigma2=sigma2, R=rho, alternative="one.sided", power=0.80)$n[1])}))
source_description: longpower vignette: Diggle-Liang-Zeger longitudinal power table

question_context: Alzheimer's Disease Phase II trial: ADAS-Cog 70-point scale, quarterly visits for 18 months (7 visits), effect=1.5 points, random intercept var=55, random slope var=24, residual var=10, slope-intercept corr=0.8. Liu-Liang method.
r_code:
sig2.i <- 55; sig2.s <- 24; sig2.e <- 10
cov.s.i <- 0.8*sqrt(sig2.i)*sqrt(sig2.s)
cov.t <- function(t1, t2, sig2.i, sig2.s, cov.s.i){ sig2.i + t1*t2*sig2.s + (t1+t2)*cov.s.i }
t <- seq(0,1.5,0.25); n <- length(t)
R <- outer(t, t, function(x,y){cov.t(x,y, sig2.i, sig2.s, cov.s.i)})
R <- R + diag(sig2.e, n, n)
u <- list(u1 = t, u2 = rep(0,n))
v <- list(v1 = cbind(1,1,t), v2 = cbind(1,0,t))
liu.liang.linear.power(d=1.5, u=u, v=v, R=R, sig.level=0.05, power=0.80)
result: N per arm = 207.3 (total ~415 subjects) for 80% power, two-sided alpha=0.05.
source_description: longpower vignette: Alzheimer's Disease trial with random slope model

question_context: Linear mixed model power from scratch: y ~ x + (1|g), 10 observations per 3 groups, fixed effects b=(2,-0.1), random intercept SD=sqrt(0.5), residual SD=1.
r_code:
x <- 1:10; g <- letters[1:3]; X <- expand.grid(x=x, g=g)
b <- c(2, -0.1); V1 <- 0.5; s <- 1
model1 <- makeLmer(y ~ x + (1|g), fixef=b, VarCorr=V1, sigma=s, data=X)
powerSim(model1, nsim=20)
result: Power for predictor x: 30.00% (95% CI: 11.89, 54.28). Effect size: -0.10. N=30 observations.
source_description: simr vignette: Power from scratch - linear mixed model

question_context: Generalized LMM power from scratch: Poisson outcome, z ~ x + (x|g), fixed effects b=(2,-0.1), random effects variance matrix V=((0.5,0.05),(0.05,0.1)).
r_code:
V2 <- matrix(c(0.5,0.05,0.05,0.1), 2)
model2 <- makeGlmer(z ~ x + (x|g), family="poisson", fixef=b, VarCorr=V2, data=X)
powerSim(model2, nsim=20)
result: Power for predictor x: 35.00% (95% CI: 15.39, 59.22). Effect size: -0.10. N=30 observations.
source_description: simr vignette: Power from scratch - Poisson GLMM

question_context: Contagious bovine pleuropneumonia data: binomial GLMM testing effect of herd size on disease incidence, with effect set to 0.05.
r_code:
gm2 <- glmer(cbind(incidence, size - incidence) ~ period + size + (1 | herd), data=cbpp, family=binomial)
fixef(gm2)["size"] <- 0.05
powerSim(gm2, fixed("size", "z"), nsim=50)
source_description: simr vignette: Binomial GLMM power for continuous predictor

question_context: Sleep study: testing whether random slope for Days significantly improves model fit over random intercept only.
r_code:
fm1 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data=sleepstudy)
doTest(fm1, compare(~ Days + (1 | Subject)))
result: Likelihood ratio test p-value = 7.07e-10 (highly significant random slope).
source_description: simr vignette: Testing random effects structure in sleepstudy

question_context: Overview of rpact workflow for adaptive clinical trial design: getDesignGroupSequential() -> getSampleSizeMeans()/getPowerMeans() -> getSimulationMeans() -> getAnalysisResults().
r_code:
design <- getDesignGroupSequential()
getSampleSizeMeans(design)
getPowerMeans(design)
getSimulationMeans(design)
result: Vignette is a workflow overview directing to rpact.org/vignettes for detailed examples. Supports fixed/adaptive, continuous/binary/survival/count endpoints, multi-arm/enrichment designs.
source_description: rpact vignette: Getting started - workflow overview (detailed examples at rpact.org/vignettes)
